one_line_takeaway: A finance-specific SLR (330 screened -> 12 retained) plus 6 interviews and 14 survey responses show AI-driven CTI in banking is stuck in an exploratory, license-driven, low-trust state, and the authors reframe trustworthy deployment as a security problem with four failure modes and three governance safeguards.
key_finding: There is a persistent translation gap: AI shows benchmark promise but almost no operational proof in financial CTI. In the SLR corpus, 33% of the 12 papers report accuracy gains over rule-based baselines on finance-CTI tasks, 17% propose sector-tailored architectures but only 8% report any pilot/operational deployment, and human factors and AI-security are each addressed by only 17%. Practitioners confirm AI is used exploratorily and often informally, with analysts still relying on rule-based correlation because AI outputs are not transparent or audit-defensible. The four synthesized failure modes are: (i) shadow AI in the wild, (ii) a license-first trap, (iii) an attacker-perception gap, and (iv) missing security for the AI itself (weak drift monitoring, adversarial testing, and audit documentation). Survey signals: 71.4% expect AI to become dominant in finance cybersecurity within five years, yet trust in AI recommendations is rated a major challenge by 50% (significant by 28.6%), and 35.7% report direct adversarial encounters. The paper concludes with three safeguards: controlled analyst-in-the-loop operation with auditable rationale and an approved internal LLM gateway; security assurance gates plus continuous drift/anomaly monitoring treating AI as an attackable system; and attack-surface reduction via capability-driven, disabled-by-default enablement of vendor AI features.
core_contribution: A mixed-methods, practitioner-centric study of why AI-driven Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) is rarely deployed in production inside financial institutions, despite regulatory pressure and vendor enthusiasm. The paper's OWN contribution has three parts: (1) a finance-specific systematic literature review (SLR) that screened 330 publications (2019-2025) and retained 12, producing what the authors call the first domain-specific map of 'AI in financial CTI' and showing the literature is benchmark-heavy and operations-light; (2) an empirical combination of six semi-structured practitioner interviews and fourteen survey responses from banks and consultancies (the authors claim this is the first such mixed-methods combination applied to AI adoption in financial CTI); and (3) a synthesis into four recurring socio-technical failure modes plus three concrete, security-oriented operational safeguards. Note: the extensive descriptions of fraud/phishing/network-anomaly ML models (e.g., refs [2,16,20,44]) are the REVIEWED prior work being characterized, not techniques the authors build or evaluate themselves.
threat_model: Not a single-adversary technical threat model; the paper frames trustworthy AI-CTI deployment as a security governance problem. The relevant threats it surfaces from practitioners are: (a) 'shadow AI' data leakage - analysts pasting sensitive incident context into unapproved public LLMs, creating unvetted data-handling paths; (b) adversarial manipulation of AI models used defensively (model evasion, data poisoning) - 35.7% of survey respondents reported direct encounters; (c) offensive attacker use of AI (e.g., phishing generation, evasion) that defenders under-weight (the 'attacker-perception gap'); (d) misuse of customer-facing AI (chatbots manipulated to disclose sensitive info) - 50% very concerned, 28.6% already encountered; and (e) attack-surface expansion from license-bundled AI features enabled without integration ('license-first trap'). The unifying claim is that analyst-AI interaction and the AI model itself must be treated as security boundaries, not usability or performance concerns.
limitations: The authors themselves flag: (1) very small, non-random, self-selected samples (n=6 interviews, n=14 survey) from mid-to-large regulated organizations, so proportions are 'indicative signals' not population estimates and transferability is bounded; (2) only 12 SLR papers retained, and an SLR window (2019-2025) that emphasizes peer-reviewed work and omits gray/vendor literature and late-2025 developments; (3) self-reported, coarse survey constructs ('AI use', 'adversarial encounter') and NDA-limited disclosure depth; residual coder bias despite high kappa. Additional reviewer-noted issues: the survey sample mixes in Energy/Education/IT/Professional-Services respondents (Table 3) rather than being purely finance, used only for governance-style triangulation. There is also an internal numerical inconsistency: the abstract states 57.1% report 'infrequent current use' and 28.6% report direct adversarial encounters, whereas the body (Fig. 1a, Fig. 3b) reports 57.1% DAILY use and 35.7% direct adversarial encounters - the body figures were used here. The three safeguards are argued from qualitative evidence and are not empirically evaluated.

datasets_used: Systematic literature review corpus: 330 records screened (2019-2025), 12 finance-relevant peer-reviewed studies retained (Table 1 evidence matrix)
Five scholarly databases searched (IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, SpringerLink, Elsevier ScienceDirect, AIS) plus NDSS and USENIX Security proceedings
Six semi-structured practitioner interviews (banking and finance-focused consulting, Feb-Mar 2025)
Exploratory survey with 14 complete responses (banks, consultancies, and a few adjacent-sector respondents)
No machine-learning training/benchmark dataset - this is a qualitative/SLR study, not a model-building paper
quantitative_results: SLR: 330 records screened, 12 retained; SLR inter-coder Cohen's kappa = 0.95

SLR characterization: 33% of the 12 papers report accuracy gains over rule-based baselines; 17% contribute CTI lifecycles/taxonomies; 25% present longitudinal threat-trend analyses; 17% propose sector-tailored platforms, of which only 8% report a pilot/deployment; human factors 17%; AI-specific risks 17%
Survey AI use (Fig. 1a): 57.1% daily, 21.4% weekly, 0% monthly, 14.3% rarely, 7.1% never (abstract instead labels this 'infrequent use' - internal inconsistency)
Importance of CTI (Fig. 1b): 50.0% critical, 35.7% very important, 14.3% moderately important
AI dominance within 5 years (Fig. 1c): 71.4% yes, 28.6% not sure, 0% no
Data quality/bias barrier (Fig. 2a): 42.9% major, 35.7% significant challenge
Human trust barrier (Fig. 2b / Fig. 3a): 50.0% major, 28.6% significant, 21.4% moderate
Regulatory-explainability concern (Fig. 2c): 33.3% very concerned, 58.3% somewhat concerned
Adversarial AI experience (Fig. 3b): 35.7% direct encounters, 57.1% aware but no experience, 7.1% no exposure
Customer-facing AI misuse concern (Fig. 3c): 50.0% very concerned, 21.4% somewhat concerned, 28.6% already encountered
baselines_compared: No algorithmic baselines - the paper is a mixed-methods SLR/interview/survey study, not an ML evaluation

As a qualitative contrast, the reviewed literature's own comparison is AI/ML models (supervised/deep learning) vs. traditional rule-based detection systems on finance-CTI tasks (card-fraud screening, phishing forecasting, network-anomaly detection), where 33% of reviewed papers report AI accuracy gains
